1: Aramaic continues (6:1–18). śām ṭəʿēm Dāryāwēš — 'King Darius issued a decree' (lit. 'placed a ṭəʿēm'). The verb śîm ṭəʿēm has appeared repeatedly (4:21; 5:3,9,13); now it is Darius himself placing the operative decree. bêt sifrayyāʾ — 'the house of archives/records.' Persian records were kept at multiple royal residences; Darius orders a search of the Babylonian archives, separate from the Ecbatana archives where the scroll is found.
2: ʾAḥməṯāʾ — Ecbatana (modern Hamadan, Iran), the Median summer capital of the Achaemenid kings. The scroll is found there — not in Babylon where the search was ordered — which is historically plausible; Cyrus spent considerable time in Media after conquering Babylon. megillāh — 'scroll' (Aramaic; = Hebrew məgillāh, H4040). The same word for the scroll of Esther (megillat Esther). The Cyrus decree exists on an actual archival scroll, not just in memory.
3: šənaṯ ḥăḏāh — 'first year of Cyrus' (538/7 BCE). The decree itself is now quoted directly: bêṯ ʾĕlāhāʾ yitbənēʾ — 'let the house of God be built.' The building specifications — 'height 60 cubits, width 60 cubits' — are given in Aramaic cubit measurements. These are substantially larger than the second temple actually became (Herod's later enlargement reached this scale), suggesting either the decree was programmatic rather than prescriptive, or a textual variant in numbers.
4: nidbāḵîn di-ʾeḇen gəlāl — 'courses of large dressed stone.' Nidbāḵ (Aramaic, H5073) = a course or layer of stones in construction. Three stone courses alternating with one timber course was standard ANE mudbrick/stone hybrid construction (cf. 1 Kgs 6:36; 7:12 for Solomon's temple using the same technique). min-bêṯ malkāʾ yittyəhēb — 'from the royal treasury shall it be paid' — the Persian crown underwrites the rebuilding. Persian royal patronage of foreign temples is confirmed by the Cyrus Cylinder.
5: ʾănayyāʾ ḏî-ḏahabāʾ wəḵəsāp — 'the gold and silver vessels.' The vessels are named again (cf. 5:14-15); their restoration to Jerusalem is a central act of the Cyrus decree. taḵtāhôn — 'in their place' (lit. 'under them'); the Aramaic preposition taḥat (H8460) = under/in place of. The vessels must be returned to their original location — the language of precise restoration rather than generic return.
6: kəʿat — 'now/therefore' (Aramaic; transitional from the citation of Cyrus's decree to Darius's own decree). Darius addresses Tattenai directly: rəḥîqîn hěwô min-tammāh — 'keep away from there' (lit. 'be distant from that place'). The command reverses ch. 4's enforcement: what the adversaries had stopped, the king's decree now protects. The same imperial authority that was weaponized against building is now weaponized for building.
7: šəḇûqû laʿăḇîḏaṯ bêṯ-ʾĕlāhāʾ ḏēn — 'leave alone the work of this house of God.' Aramaic šəḇaq (H7662) = to leave/permit/allow. The same root appears in the Lord's Prayer (Aramaic šəḇaq = 'forgive/release'). pəḥaṯ yəhûḏāyēʾ — 'the governor of the Jews' — authority is delegated to the local Jewish governor (Zerubbabel) and elders; Persian oversight withdraws. The verb construction is imperatival: Darius commands Tattenai's non-interference.
8: miḏatṯ ʾăḵəsfarnāyāʾ — 'from the royal revenues' (miḏāh, H4061, is the tribute/tax term from 4:13). The reverse of the adversaries' argument: they warned that Jerusalem's rebuilding would reduce tax revenue; now Darius orders that tax revenue fund the rebuilding. The fiscal argument is flipped. ḇə-ʾosparnāʾ — 'diligently, without delay' (H629); the same adverb appears in 5:8 and 7:17,21,26 — a key Aramaic administrative term for urgent, proper execution of orders.
9: ʿănê ṯôrîn — 'young bulls' (Aramaic ʿanê, H6450). The list of sacrificial materials — bulls, rams, lambs, wheat, salt, wine, oil — mirrors Levitical requirements (Lev 1–7). That Darius specifies exact sacrificial requirements reflects Persian religious policy: to supply the needs of subject-peoples' temples in exchange for prayers for the imperial household. millāḥ — salt (H4416); salt is both a preservative and a covenant marker (Lev 2:13; Num 18:19; cf. Ezra 4:14 'we eat the king's salt').
10: liqrāḇāh nîḥôḥîn — 'to offer acceptable sacrifices' (lit. 'to offer pleasing-aroma sacrifices'). Aramaic nîḥôaḥ (H5208) = pleasant aroma — the Aramaic form of Hebrew nîḥôaḥ (H5207), the same word used throughout Leviticus for offerings acceptable to YHWH (Lev 1:9,13,17 etc.). The pagan king uses the technical sacrificial vocabulary. wəyitṣallôn — 'and pray' (Aramaic pael of ṣlh, H6739); the exchange is explicit: Persian money → Jewish sacrifices and prayers → divine protection of Persia. Pragmatic polytheism from the Persian side; genuine worship from the Jewish side.
11: ʾîlān mêbiṯēh — 'a beam pulled from his house' — the death penalty is expressed in construction language (a beam pulled from his house), then the offender is 'impaled on it' (məmḥēʾ ʿălôhî). This is the standard Persian royal decree formula for capital offenses, attested in other Achaemenid inscriptions. ûnְwālî yitʿəḇad — 'and his house made a dunghill/ruin.' The complete reversal: from house-owner to impaled-on-his-own-beam, house to dunghill. The severity underscores that Darius's protection of the temple is binding, not optional.
12: wêʾĕlāhāʾ ḏî-šakkan šəmēh tamāh — 'the God who caused his name to dwell there.' This is the most theologically significant verse in the Aramaic section. Šakkan šəmēh is the Aramaic exact equivalent of the Deuteronomic formula šikkēn šəmô (Deut 12:5,11; 14:23; 16:2,6,11; 26:2) — the theology of divine name-presence in the sanctuary. That this Deuteronomic formula appears in an Aramaic imperial decree attributed to Darius is remarkable: either Darius's scribes adapted Jewish theological language, or the text was edited by the Chronicler to embed the name-theology. Either way, the Chronicler places YHWH's name-presence at the center of his decree — the temple is the place where YHWH's name dwells, and any king who interferes with it opposes YHWH himself.
13: ʾōsparnāʾ — 'diligently/exactly' (H629, same as v8). Tattenai's compliance is described with the same adverb that Darius used for his command — word-for-word obedience. The narrative signals that imperial opposition to the covenant community has completely reversed: the same officials who threatened to stop the building (4:23–24) now fully comply with its protection. kəmāʾ ḏî śām Dāryāwēš — 'exactly as Darius had decreed' — the verbal echo closes the loop.
14: wūbənô … wəṣliḥû bənăḇûʾaṯ ḥaggay — 'they built and prospered through the prophecy of Haggai.' Three Aramaic verbs in sequence: bənô (built), ṣliḥû (prospered/succeeded), šaklilû (finished). The middle verb ṣlāḥ (H6744) = to prosper, succeed — it is YHWH's blessing on the activity, not just human effort. bəmiṣwaṯ ʾĕlāh-yiśrāʾēl ûmiṭṭəʿēm Kôreš ûDāryāwēš — 'by the command of the God of Israel and the decree of Cyrus, Darius, and Artaxerxes.' The Chronicler deliberately merges divine command and imperial decree: three Persian kings and the one God of Israel all concur in the building.
15: wayyiḵlēʾ bêṯāʾ — 'the house was completed' (Aramaic klāʾ, H3635 = to complete). The date: third day of Adar, sixth year of Darius = March 12, 516 BCE. The significance of this date is striking: if the temple was destroyed in 586 BCE (2 Kgs 25:8–9), the second temple's completion in 516 BCE falls exactly 70 years later — the duration of Jeremiah's predicted desolation (Jer 25:11–12; 29:10; cf. Zech 1:12; Dan 9:2). The 70-year structure is not incidental; the Chronicler (and Darius's decree by implication) positions the temple completion as the fulfillment of Jeremiah's prophecy.
16: Hebrew resumes at 6:16. ḥănukkāh — 'dedication' (H2598 ḥăṇukkāh; same word as the later Hanukkah festival, itself a second-temple dedication celebration). bənê-yiśrāʾēl — 'sons of Israel' — the covenant-community name deliberately recalls the exodus narrative. The dedication is celebrated with joy (bəśimḥāh). The return from exile is implicitly a new exodus; the temple dedication recapitulates Sinai-era consecration.
17: ḥaṭṭāʾt ləḵol-yiśrāʾēl šənêm-ʿāśār śəʿîrê ʿizzîm — 'twelve male goats as a sin offering for all Israel' — twelve goats for the twelve tribes. That only returned exiles (principally Judah and Benjamin, with some Levites) perform this offering on behalf of 'all Israel' is a theological claim: the restoration community stands as the representative of the whole covenant people. The twelve-tribe symbolism had been claimed at Zerubbabel's altar (3:1–2); it is now reiterated in the temple dedication.
18: wayaʿămiḏû — 'they appointed/stationed' (hiphil of ʿāmaḏ, H5975). The priestly and Levitical divisions are established kəḵeṯaḇ sēper Mōšeh — 'as written in the book of Moses.' The explicit appeal to the Mosaic text grounds the restored worship in Torah. The Chronicler consistently emphasizes that authentic worship requires Mosaic authorization (cf. 2 Chr 35:26). The institutional structure of the temple (divisions, courses, orders) is the embodiment of covenantal order.
19: wayyaʿăśû ḇənê-haggôlāh ʾeṯ-happāsaḥ — 'the returned exiles observed the Passover.' The Passover (pāsaḥ, H6453) is the covenant-renewal meal par excellence — it marks the founding of Israel as a redeemed community (Exod 12). Its observance here signals that the exile is formally over: the community stands in the same position as the exodus generation, re-constituted as YHWH's people in the land. The Passover connects the restored community to the original deliverance.
20: kî ṭihărû yāḥaḏ — 'for they had all purified themselves together.' The ritual purity is thorough and corporate (yāḥaḏ). Priestly purity enabled them to slaughter the Passover lamb 'for all the returned exiles' — the priests served the whole community. The hiphil verb šəḥāṭû (slaughtered) indicates the priests performing the sacrifice on behalf of laity, a Second Temple innovation from the norm of family-slaughter in Exod 12.
21: niḇdəlû meṭumʾat gôyê hāʾāreṣ — 'who had separated themselves from the impurity of the nations of the land.' The phrase is significant: the Passover includes not only the returned exiles but also those who 'joined them' by separating from syncretistic practice. This is an inclusionary provision — non-exilic Israelites (who had remained in the land) could participate in the covenant community by ritual separation from pagan contamination. The theological principle: covenant membership is defined by commitment to YHWH, not only by exile-and-return history.
22: wayyəśabbēr YHWH ʾet-libbam meleḵ ʾaššûr — 'the LORD turned the heart of the king of Assyria toward them.' The king is called 'king of Assyria' though he is Darius/Artaxerxes — the Chronicler uses 'Assyria' as a symbolic name for the dominant gentile power (as in Neh 9:32). hāsēb ʾeṯ-libbô — lit. 'turned his heart' — the same verb and idiom as 2 Chr 36:22 (Cyrus's heart 'stirred' by YHWH) and Prov 21:1 ('the king's heart is in YHWH's hand'). The confession that closes the dedication narrative: the imperial authorization was divinely orchestrated.
